## What went wrong

The red bar shows the app calling **`http://localhost:8081/auth_login.php`**. In the browser, **localhost is the visitor’s PC**, not your droplet, so the request fails.

Your Flutter **release** build bakes in `API_BASE_URL` at compile time (see `AppConfigService` → `_productionApiBase`). Your server log shows:

`CACHED [web build-env 6/6] RUN flutter build web ...`

So Docker **never re-ran** the Flutter build: an **old image** is still in use—one that was built when the API URL was `localhost:8081` (or similar). `docker compose up -d --build web` does **not** invalidate that layer if inputs look unchanged to Docker.

## What we changed in the repo

1. **`docker-compose.yml`** — `API_URL` is now **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`** so it matches users hitting **HTTPS** on Cloudflare (plain **HTTP** API calls from an **HTTPS** admin page are often blocked as mixed content).
2. **`Dockerfile.web`** — default `API_URL` is the same HTTPS URL, plus a short comment to use **`--no-cache`** when this changes.
3. **`app_config_service.dart`** and **`api_client.dart`** — production fallbacks use **HTTPS** for the same reason.

Push/pull these changes to the server (or copy the files), then rebuild the web image **without cache** and restart:

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
docker compose build --no-cache web
docker compose up -d web
```

(Optional but thorough: `docker compose build --no-cache` for everything.)

## Cloudflare / DNS checklist

- **`api.medtechinventorysystem.org`** must be a DNS record (usually **A** to `159.223.47.185`, or a **CNAME** if you use another name).
- Same for **`admin.medtechinventorysystem.org`**.
- After deploy, open **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/ping.php`** in the browser; you should get JSON with `"ok": true` (or similar). If that fails, fix DNS/nginx before retesting login.

After a clean web rebuild, login should call **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/auth_login.php`** instead of localhost.

_**Agent (default)**_


## What went wrong

You ran:

```text
git push main origin
```

Git treats the first argument after `push` as the **remote name** and the rest as **refs**. So it tried to use a remote named `main` and push something called `origin`, which does not exist. That produces `src refspec origin does not match any`.

## Correct form

```text
git push origin main
```

- **`origin`** — your remote (GitHub: `Jayquio/inventoryQR`)
- **`main`** — the branch you want to push

That push has been run for you and completed successfully (`main -> main`).

**Tip:** `git push -u origin main` sets upstream so later you can use just `git push`.

docker compose up -d web

## Why it failed

The important line is **`write error (disk full?)`**. Your VPS root filesystem is small (~8.65 GB) and already ~82% full. A **`docker compose build --no-cache web`** run:

- Reinstalls OS packages  
- **Clones the whole Flutter repo**  
- **Downloads the Dart SDK (~222 MB)** into the image  

That can fill the remaining space. The SDK file then ends up **truncated**, so Flutter reports it as **corrupt**.

**`client_loop: send disconnect: Connection reset`** is just the SSH session dropping (network/timeout/sleep); it’s unrelated to the Docker error.

---

## What to do on the server

1. **Free space** (run as root on the droplet):

```bash
docker builder prune -af
docker system prune -af
apt-get clean
df -h /
```

Only use `docker system prune -af --volumes` if you know you don’t need unused volumes (your DB volume must stay).

2. **Pull the repo update** (after you push) so you get the new **`Dockerfile.web`**, which uses **`ghcr.io/cirruslabs/flutter:stable`** instead of cloning Flutter in the build. That usually uses **less disk and time** on tiny servers.

3. Rebuild:

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR && git pull
docker compose build --no-cache web && docker compose up -d web
```

If it still fails, **resize the droplet disk** or **build the web app on GitHub Actions / your PC** and only deploy the built static files.

---

I’ve updated your local **`Dockerfile.web`** to use the Cirrus Flutter image and a `pubspec` + `pubspec.lock` layer for caching. **Commit and push**, then `git pull` on the server before rebuilding.

_**Agent (default)**_


Here’s how to read what you’re seeing.

## GitHub Actions: green = “SSH script finished with success”

Your workflow (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) runs on the server:

```21:25:c:\3rd Year\SEM 2\SIA 2\QR CODE INVENTORY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM\inventoryQR\.github\workflows\deploy.yml
          script: |
            cd ~/inventoryQR
            git pull origin main
            docker compose down
            docker compose up -d --build
```

For a normal setup, if **`docker compose up -d --build`** fails (including a failed image build), the SSH step should get a non-zero exit code and the job should go **red**, not green.

So **yes: for that run, it is reasonable to treat the green check as “`git pull` + `docker compose down` + `docker compose up -d --build` all succeeded on the droplet.”** It does **not** by itself prove the app works in the browser (DNS, Cloudflare, API URL, etc. are separate).

The **1 warning** in Annotations is usually something like Node version or action deprecation; open it to see if it matters. It doesn’t cancel the green status.

---

## DigitalOcean droplet: “correct” for what you’re doing?

The panel matches what you’ve been using (hostname, **159.223.47.185**, small droplet in SGP1). So **the server and IP are consistent** with your SSH deploy.

What’s **not** ideal for your stack:

- **10 GB disk** — you already hit **disk full** during a Flutter/Docker build; that’s expected on this size if images and build cache pile up.
- **512 MB RAM** — very tight for MySQL + Redis + PHP + Nginx + Flutter build steps inside Docker; builds can be slow or flaky even when they sometimes succeed.

So: **correct machine and networking**, **undersized for comfortable builds** — better to free/prune Docker often, avoid unnecessary `--no-cache`, or build the web image elsewhere / resize the disk.

---

## Short answer

- **Workflow + green run:** For your YAML, that usually means the deploy commands on the droplet completed successfully **for that push**.
- **Droplet:** Correct instance; just **small** for full Docker + Flutter builds — plan around disk and RAM or upgrade if builds keep failing.

If something still fails in the browser after a green deploy, the next place to look is runtime (containers up, `https://api.../ping.php`, API URL baked into the web build), not whether GitHub’s checkbox is “wrong.”

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Analyzing user situation**

The user's git pull failed due to local changes in docker-compose.yml, which won't be preserved during merge - this is problematic if they want their repo to match GitHub. </details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_


**Partly okay, partly not.**

### 1. `git pull` failed — that is **not** okay

Git stopped because **`docker-compose.yml` was changed on the server** and would be overwritten. So **the droplet did not get your latest GitHub commit** (including any `Dockerfile.web` / compose fixes).

Until you fix that, you’re building **whatever is already on disk**, not necessarily what’s on `main`.

**Typical fix on the server** (only if you’re sure you don’t need those local edits):

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
git status
git diff docker-compose.yml   # see what differs
# If server edits were accidental:
git checkout -- docker-compose.yml
git pull origin main
```

If the server changes were intentional, use `git stash` or commit them, then merge/rebase — but for a deploy box, **matching GitHub** is usually what you want.

---

### 2. `docker compose build` ran **after** the failed pull

So this build used the **current server tree** (still at the old commit), **not** the updated one from GitHub.

---

### 3. Long build (~39 minutes) and `git clone` Flutter

Your log still shows **`RUN git clone ... flutter.git`** — that’s the **old** `Dockerfile.web` (Ubuntu + clone Flutter), not the slimmer Cirrus image. So the server **never pulled** the updated Dockerfile.

A **35–40+ minute** `flutter build web` on a **512 MB / 10 GB** droplet can “work” but is **heavy**; it’s expected to be slow, not a sign that something is ideal.

---

### What you should do

1. Resolve **git** on the server (stash/checkout/`git pull`) so `~/inventoryQR` matches GitHub.  
2. Then run **`docker compose build --no-cache web`** (or rely on CI `docker compose up -d --build` after push).  
3. Optionally skip `--no-cache` on that machine unless you must force a full rebuild — it saves time and disk.

**Summary:** The failed `git pull` means the deploy is **not** aligned with the repo until you fix the `docker-compose.yml` conflict. The long Flutter compile can be “normal” for that old Dockerfile on a tiny VPS, but you should get `git pull` working first.

---

step by step of this
"are consistent with your SSH deploy.

how to do the change or virtual add memory?
To handle a 512MB RAM limit on a DigitalOcean Droplet, you cannot "add" physical RAM without paying for a larger plan. However, you can create a Swap File, which uses your SSD disk space to act as "virtual memory." This prevents your MySQL and Docker builds from crashing when they run out of real RAM.
Step 1: Create a 2GB Swap File
Run these commands one by one in your SSH terminal (root@159.223.47.185):
bash
# Create a 2GB file to use for swap
sudo fallocate -l 2G /swapfile

# Set the correct permissions (only root should access it)
sudo chmod 600 /swapfile

# Set up the file as swap space
sudo mkswap /swapfile

# Enable the swap
sudo swapon /swapfile

# Verify it is working (you should see 'swap' in the list)
free -h
Use code with caution.

Step 2: Make the Swap Permanent
If you don't do this, the swap will disappear when you restart the Droplet.
Open the fstab file: sudo nano /etc/fstab
Scroll to the bottom and add this line:
/swapfile none swap sw 0 0
Save and exit (Ctrl+O, Enter, Ctrl+X).
Step 3: Clean Up Disk Space (The 10GB Limit)
Since you only have 10GB of disk and your usage was at 82%, you need to delete old Docker "junk" so the new Swap file has room to breathe.
Run this command to delete all unused images, stopped containers, and build caches:
bash
docker system prune -a --volumes
Use code with caution.

Type y when asked. This is safe; it only deletes things that are not currently running.
Step 4: Optimize Docker Builds
Because 512MB is very small, running docker compose up --build on the server might still be slow.
Pro-Tip: If your Flutter build keeps failing on the server, you should build the Flutter web folder on your laptop first, then push the build/web folder to GitHub. This way, the Droplet only has to "serve" the files instead of "compiling" them.
Does the free -h command show the 2GB of Swap now?




explain Pro-Tip: If your Flutter build keeps failing on the server, you should build the Flutter web folder on your laptop first, then push the build/web folder to GitHub. This way, the Droplet only has to "serve" the files instead of "compiling" them.
When you run docker compose up --build on your 512MB Droplet, the server tries to "compile" your Flutter code. This is like asking a tiny calculator to build a whole house—it runs out of memory and crashes.
By building on your laptop first, you do the "heavy lifting" at home and only send the finished "house" (the HTML/JS files) to the server.
The "New" Way to Update Your App
1. Build Locally on your Laptop
Open your terminal in your project folder on Windows and run:
powershell
flutter build web --release
Use code with caution.

This creates a folder called build/web containing the final website.
2. Tell Git to "Stop Ignoring" the Build
By default, Flutter projects hide the build/ folder. You need to let Git see the finished web files.
Open your .gitignore file.
Find the line that says /build/ and delete it (or put a # in front of it: #/build/).
3. Update your Dockerfile.web
Change your Dockerfile.web so it doesn't try to install Flutter on the server. It should just look like this:
dockerfile
FROM nginx:alpine
# Just copy the finished files from your laptop to the Nginx server
COPY build/web /usr/share/nginx/html
EXPOSE 80
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
Use code with caution.

4. Push Everything
powershell
git add .
git commit -m "Build web locally to save server RAM"
git push origin main
Use code with caution.

Why this fixes your problem:
Zero RAM Usage for Building: Your Droplet won't have to run the flutter command anymore. It just starts Nginx and displays the files you already made.
Disk Space: You don't need to store the heavy Flutter SDK (which is ~1-2GB) on your tiny 10GB Droplet disk.
Speed: Your GitHub Action will finish in seconds instead of 10+ minutes.
"

_**Agent (default)**_


Here’s a **step-by-step** version of that plan, with **warnings** where people often get hurt.

---

## A. “Virtual memory” on DigitalOcean (swap)

You **cannot add real RAM** on a 512 MB droplet without **resizing the droplet** (bigger plan = more RAM, costs more).

**Swap** = a file on **disk** that Linux uses like slow RAM when physical RAM is full. It helps avoid **OOM kills** and some crashes; it does **not** make the machine as fast as real RAM.

### Step A1 — Check free disk first

A **2 GB swap file** needs **2 GB free** on `/`. If the disk is almost full, create a **smaller** swap (e.g. `1G`) or free space first.

```bash
df -h /
free -h
```

### Step A2 — Create and enable swap (run in order)

```bash
sudo fallocate -l 2G /swapfile
sudo chmod 600 /swapfile
sudo mkswap /swapfile
sudo swapon /swapfile
free -h
```

You want to see **Swap** with ~2 Gi under `free -h`.

If `fallocate` fails on some systems, use:

```bash
sudo dd if=/dev/zero of=/swapfile bs=1M count=2048 status=progress
sudo chmod 600 /swapfile
sudo mkswap /swapfile
sudo swapon /swapfile
```

### Step A3 — Persist after reboot

```bash
sudo nano /etc/fstab
```

Add **one line** at the end:

```text
/swapfile none swap sw 0 0
```

Save and exit. After a reboot, `free -h` should still show swap.

### Step A4 — Optional tuning (swappiness)

Lower value = use RAM more, swap less (often nicer on small VPS):

```bash
echo 'vm.swappiness=10' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf
sudo sysctl vm.swappiness=10
```

---

## B. Free disk (10 GB limit)

### Step B1 — Safer cleanup (recommended first)

These remove **unused** Docker data **without** blindly deleting volumes:

```bash
docker builder prune -af
docker image prune -af
docker system prune -f
df -h /
```

### Step B2 — `docker system prune -a --volumes` (dangerous name)

That command **can delete named volumes** that are **not** attached to a **running** container. If your database lives in a Docker volume and the DB container is **stopped**, you could **lose the database**.

**Rule:** Only use `--volumes` when you understand what volumes you have:

```bash
docker volume ls
```

For a typical `docker compose` app with a **db volume you care about**, prefer **not** using `--volumes` unless you have backups or you’re sure.

---

## C. Avoid unnecessary `--no-cache`

- **`docker compose up -d --build`** — rebuilds when Dockerfile/context changed; reuses layers when possible. **Use this for normal deploys.**
- **`docker compose build --no-cache web`** — forces a full rebuild; **max disk/RAM/time**. Use only when you must (e.g. wrong `API_BASE_URL` baked in and cache won’t refresh).

---

## D. Resize disk / RAM (real upgrade)

In **DigitalOcean control panel**: **Droplet → Resize** (or create a larger droplet). That’s the only way to get **more real RAM** or **more disk** officially. Swap is a **supplement**, not a replacement.

---

## E. Pro-tip explained: build Flutter on laptop, server only serves files

### What happens today

On the server, **`Dockerfile.web`** runs **`flutter build web` inside Docker**. That needs:

- Flutter SDK + Dart + compile — **lots of RAM and disk**  
- On 512 MB / 10 GB, that’s why builds are **slow** or **fail**

### The idea

1. On your **Windows PC** (with Flutter installed), you run **`flutter build web --release`** once.  
2. That produces **`build/web/`** — plain **HTML, JS, assets** (no Flutter toolchain needed to “run” them).  
3. The server’s **`Dockerfile.web`** becomes **only nginx** + **`COPY build/web`** into `/usr/share/nginx/html`.  
4. On deploy, Docker **does not compile** Flutter; it only copies static files → **tiny RAM/disk** usage on the droplet.

### Tradeoffs (honest)

- You must pass the right API URL at **local** build time, e.g.  
  `flutter build web --release --dart-define=API_BASE_URL=https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`  
  (same idea as Docker’s `API_URL`).
- **Committing `build/web` to Git** makes the repo bigger and mixes “source” with “built output”; some teams use **GitHub Actions** to build web and deploy the artifact instead (same benefit, no binary folder in Git).
- You need to adjust **`.gitignore`**: if `/build/` is ignored, Git won’t include `build/web` until you change that (or use CI to build and rsync/scp without committing).

### Why it fixes your problem

| On droplet | Full Flutter in Docker | Pre-built `build/web` only |
|------------|-------------------------|----------------------------|
| Compile    | Yes (heavy)             | No                         |
| RAM peak   | High                    | Low                        |
| Disk       | SDK + caches            | Mostly static files        |
| Deploy time| Long                    | Short                      |

---

## F. Order that usually works

1. **`df -h` / `free -h`** — know disk/RAM.  
2. **Free Docker disk** (safe prune first; avoid `--volumes` unless you’re sure).  
3. **Add swap** (1–2 G) if you still build on server.  
4. **Fix `git pull`** on server so code matches GitHub.  
5. Long term: **resize droplet** and/or **build web off the droplet** (laptop or GitHub Actions).

---

**Direct answer:** **`free -h` after `swapon`** should show **Swap** ~2 Gi if swap was created and enabled successfully. If **Swap** is still 0, the `swapon` step failed or the file wasn’t created—check for errors in the commands above.

I’m in **Ask mode**, so I can’t edit your repo or run commands on your machine; if you want the Dockerfile / `.gitignore` / workflow changed in the project, switch to **Agent mode** and ask for that explicitly.

Here’s what was set up and how to use it.

---

## 1. How you know you’re on the **DigitalOcean** box

After you SSH in:

| Check | What it means |
|--------|----------------|
| `hostname` | Should match the droplet name (e.g. `ubuntu-s-1vcpu-512mb-10gb-sgp1-01`). |
| `curl -s ifconfig.me` or `ip a` | Public IPv4 should be **`159.223.47.185`** (same as in the DO dashboard). |
| `ls ~/inventoryQR` | Your deployed project folder is there. |

If those match your DigitalOcean **Access** page, you’re on the deployed server.

---

## 2. What we changed (option **E**)

- **`Dockerfile.web`** — Only **nginx** + `COPY build/web`. No Flutter on the droplet.
- **`.gitignore`** — Ignores `/build/*` **except** `build/web/` (so the bundle can be committed).
- **`.dockerignore`** — Same idea so Docker gets `build/web` in the build context.
- **`docker-compose.yml`** — Removed unused `API_URL` build args for `web`.
- **`scripts/build_web_prod.ps1`** — One command to build with the right API URL.
- **`README.md`** — Short deploy + “am I on the right server?” notes.
- **`build/web/`** — Built locally with  
  `--dart-define=API_BASE_URL=https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`  
  and **committed + pushed** to `main` (`b6266f2`).

---

## 3. Your routine from here

**When you change the app or API URL:**

1. In the project folder on your PC:
   ```powershell
   .\scripts\build_web_prod.ps1
   ```
2. Commit and push:
   ```powershell
   git add build/web
   git commit -m "Refresh web bundle"
   git push origin main
   ```
3. Deploy: GitHub Actions will SSH and run `docker compose up -d --build`, or you SSH and:
   ```bash
   cd ~/inventoryQR
   git pull origin main
   docker compose up -d --build
   ```

The **web** image build should finish in **seconds** on the droplet (only copying static files).

---

## 4. If `git pull` fails on the server again

You still have **local edits** to `docker-compose.yml` on the droplet. To **throw them away** and match GitHub:

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
git checkout -- docker-compose.yml
git pull origin main
docker compose up -d --build
```

---

## 5. Optional check from your PC (no SSH)

- Open **`https://admin.medtechinventorysystem.org`** — admin UI (through Cloudflare).
- Open **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/ping.php`** — API should respond with JSON.

That confirms the **public** deployment; SSH checks confirm you’re on the **actual** VM.

---

**Note:** Committing `build/web` makes the repo larger; for a class project that’s fine. If Docker Desktop is stopped on your PC, local `docker compose build web` will fail until you start it—the droplet doesn’t need that for this flow.

Your latest changes are already **`git push origin main`**; the next Action run should pull the new `Dockerfile` and `build/web` and rebuild a lightweight `web` image.

---

Here’s what’s going on and what to do.

---

## You **are** on the right machine

`hostname` and `159.223.47.185` match your DigitalOcean droplet. **`~/inventoryQR` is correct** for deploy commands.

---

## Why you see **Cloudflare 521**

521 means Cloudflare **could not open a connection** to your origin (or got refused). Typical causes with your setup:

### 1. **Cloudflare SSL mode** (very likely)

Your `nginx.prod.conf` only has **`listen 80`** — no HTTPS on the droplet.

- If Cloudflare is set to **Full** or **Full (strict)**, it tries **HTTPS to port 443** on `159.223.47.185`. Nothing is listening there → **521**.
- **Fix:** In Cloudflare go to **SSL/TLS → Overview** and set encryption to **Flexible** (visitor ↔ Cloudflare stays HTTPS; Cloudflare ↔ your server uses **HTTP on port 80**).

*(Later you can move to Full by adding TLS on the droplet; Flexible is the quick fix that matches “nginx on 80 only”.)

### 2. **Docker stack stopped**

Your old workflow did **`docker compose down`** and then **`up`**. If **`up --build` failed** (e.g. missing `build/web`, disk full), **nothing** would be left running → **521**.

**On the server (SSH), run:**

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
git pull origin main
docker compose ps
docker compose up -d --build --remove-orphans
docker compose ps
```

**Sanity check from the same server:**

```bash
curl -sI -H "Host: admin.medtechinventorysystem.org" http://127.0.0.1/
```

You want a real response (e.g. **200** or a redirect), not “Connection refused”.

### 3. **Missing `build/web` on the droplet**

Your `ls` listing did **not** show a **`build/`** directory. The current **`Dockerfile.web`** needs **`build/web`**. If the server is behind `main`, run **`git pull origin main`** and confirm:

```bash
ls ~/inventoryQR/build/web/index.html
```

If that file is missing, pull failed or the repo on disk is old — fix git (e.g. `git checkout -- docker-compose.yml` / resolve conflicts) then pull again.

---

## Your **Windows** terminal mistakes (from your log)

| What you ran | Problem |
|----------------|--------|
| `cd ~/inventoryQR` in PowerShell | **`~` is not your project** on Windows; use the full path to `inventoryQR` on your PC. |
| `docker compose up` on Windows | Fails if **Docker Desktop** is **off** (`dockerDesktopLinuxEngine` error). Deploy to production by **SSH on the droplet** or **GitHub Actions**, not necessarily Docker on your laptop. |

---

## Repo change (already pushed)

- **Deploy workflow** no longer runs **`docker compose down`** before **`up`**, so a failed build won’t leave the site completely down.
- **README** now has a **Cloudflare 521** section and a **Windows vs SSH** note.

Commit: **`9f365f4`** on `main`.

---

**Fast path:** set Cloudflare SSL to **Flexible**, then SSH and run **`git pull`** + **`docker compose up -d --build --remove-orphans`**, then **`curl`** test above. If anything errors, paste that terminal output.

---

_**User**_

maybe clean the docker first?

---

_**Agent (default)**_


Yes — **cleaning Docker on the droplet first** is often a good idea on a **10 GB** disk, especially after failed or huge Flutter builds. It frees space so `docker compose up -d --build` can pull layers and finish.

### Safer order (keeps your database volume)

Run **on the server over SSH** (not on Windows unless you mean local Docker):

1. **Build cache** (usually safe, big win):
   ```bash
   docker builder prune -af
   ```

2. **Stopped containers + unused networks + dangling images** (safe, no named volumes removed):
   ```bash
   docker system prune -af
   ```

3. **Check space:**
   ```bash
   df -h /
   docker system df
   ```

Then:
```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR && git pull origin main && docker compose up -d --build --remove-orphans
```

### When **not** to use aggressive cleanup

Avoid **`docker system prune -a --volumes`** unless you know what you’re doing. **`--volumes`** can delete **unused** named volumes; if your DB container wasn’t running, you could lose **MySQL data** in `db_data`.

If you only want to remove **unused** images after a successful deploy, the two commands above are usually enough.

### Summary

- **Yes**, prune **builder cache** and **`docker system prune -af`** on the droplet before rebuilding.  
- **Skip `--volumes`** unless you’ve confirmed backups or you don’t care about the DB volume.

---

_**User**_

then what next?

---

_**Agent (default)**_


After you’ve pruned Docker on the **droplet** (builder prune + `docker system prune -af`, **without** `--volumes` unless you mean to wipe DB data), do this in order:

### 1. Pull latest code
```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
git pull origin main
```
If git complains about local changes to `docker-compose.yml`, either save them elsewhere or discard with `git checkout -- docker-compose.yml`, then `git pull` again.

### 2. Confirm `build/web` exists (needed for your current `Dockerfile.web`)
```bash
test -f build/web/index.html && echo OK || echo "Missing build/web — pull failed or old commit"
```

### 3. Bring the stack up
```bash
docker compose up -d --build --remove-orphans
```

### 4. Check containers
```bash
docker compose ps
```
Everything you care about (especially `nginx`, `web`, `api`, `db`) should be **running** / **healthy**.

```bash

```
You want HTTP responses, not “connection refused”.

- `https://admin.medtechinventorysystem.org`
- If you still get **521**, set Cloudflare **SSL/TLS** to **Flexible** (your nginx only listens on **port 80** unless you’ve added TLS on the droplet).

Push any change to `main` or re-run the **Deploy to DigitalOcean** workflow so the server pulls and runs compose again.

That’s the full “clean → deploy → verify” path. If a step fails, the **exact error line** from that step is what to fix next.

The blocker is **only Git**: the server has **local edits** to `docker-compose.yml` that differ from GitHub, so `git pull` refuses to run.

Throw away the server’s copy of that file, then pull:

```bash
docker compose ps
```

**Summary:** Run **`git checkout -- docker-compose.yml`** (if you don’t need server-only edits), then **`git pull`**, then **`docker compose up -d --build --remove-orphans`**.

_**Agent (default)**_


You’re probably asking about **one of these two** things from your last session:

---

### 1. **`error: Your local changes ... would be overwritten by merge: docker-compose.yml`**

That’s **Git** telling you:

- On the **droplet**, someone (or you) **edited** `docker-compose.yml` in `~/inventoryQR`.
- **GitHub** has a **different** `docker-compose.yml`.
- Git **won’t** run `git pull` because it would **overwrite** those local edits and you’d lose them without warning.

So “this” is **not** a Docker error — it’s **Git protecting you** from losing the server’s version of that file.

**Fix if the server should match GitHub:** discard the server copy of that file, then pull:

`git checkout -- docker-compose.yml` then `git pull origin main`.

---

### 2. **`docker system df` / “Images 0, Containers 0”**

That’s **Docker’s disk report** after you pruned:

- **No images / containers** = nothing is built or running right now; you need **`docker compose up -d --build`** again after `git pull` succeeds.
- **Local volumes** = named disks (often your **database** data). They can show **0 active** when no container is using them yet; that doesn’t always mean the data was deleted (unless you ran prune **with** `--volumes`).

---

If “this” is something else (a line in the terminal, a screenshot, a setting), paste the **exact text** or image and say which line you mean.

_**Agent (default)**_


That output is a normal **`git status`** summary.

### “Your branch is up to date with `origin/main`”
Your clone on the server matches **GitHub’s `main`** for **tracked** files. You’re good to run **`docker compose up -d --build`** if you haven’t already.

### “Untracked files: `nginx.prod.conf.save`, `nginx.prod.conf.save.1`”
Those are **extra files on disk** that Git is **not** tracking. They’re almost certainly **backups** someone made while editing `nginx.prod.conf` (e.g. editor auto-backup or `cp nginx.prod.conf nginx.prod.conf.save`).

- They **do not** affect Docker unless you **rename** them over the real config.
- Docker uses whatever is in **`nginx.prod.conf`** as referenced in **`docker-compose.yml`**.
- You can **ignore** them, **delete** them if you don’t need the backups, or **leave** them — they won’t change your deploy by themselves.

### Do you need to `git add` them?
**No**, unless you *want* those backups in the repo (you usually **don’t**).

**Next step on the server:** if the stack isn’t up yet, run:

```bash
docker compose up -d --build --remove-orphans
docker compose ps
```

That red bar means the **JavaScript running in your browser** is still calling **`http://localhost:8081/auth_login.php`**. The browser treats “localhost” as **your own PC**, not the droplet, so login will always fail with “Failed to fetch.”

So this is **not** a password problem — it’s the **wrong API base URL inside the built web app** (or an **old copy** of the app being served).

Also double-check the hostname: the screenshot shows **`admin.meditechinventorysystem.org`** (extra **e**: *meditech*). Your project and earlier messages used **`medtechinventorysystem.org`**. If that domain is real, it might be a **different** deployment or old files. Make sure the URL matches the site you actually updated.

---

### 1. Confirm what the server is serving

SSH to the droplet:

```bash
grep -o 'localhost:8081' ~/inventoryQR/build/web/main.dart.js | head -1
grep -o 'api.medtechinventorysystem.org' ~/inventoryQR/build/web/main.dart.js | head -1
```

- If **`localhost:8081` appears** → the **committed `build/web`** on the server is still an old bundle. You need a **new** `flutter build web` with the production API URL and to **deploy that** `build/web`.
- If **`api.medtech...` appears** and **not** localhost → the file on disk is fine; then the problem is almost certainly **browser or CDN cache** (step 3).

---

### 2. Fix the bundle (on your Windows machine, in the project folder)

```powershell
flutter pub get
flutter build web --release --dart-define=API_BASE_URL=https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org
```

Then commit and push **`build/web`** (or let your usual workflow push it), pull on the server, rebuild the **`web`** image / restart compose:

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR && git pull && docker compose up -d --build web
```

(Or full `docker compose up -d --build` if you prefer.)

Use the **same** API host you expose publicly (must match Cloudflare/DNS), e.g. **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`**.

---

### 3. Clear cached app in the browser

Flutter web uses a **service worker**; it can keep **old `main.dart.js`**:

- DevTools → **Application** → **Service Workers** → **Unregister**, then hard reload  
  or  
- Clear **site data** for `admin.…` and reload.

Try again in a **private window** after deploy.

---

### 4. Quick sanity check

Open:

`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/ping.php`  
(substitute your real API host if different)

If that works in the browser but login still hits **localhost:8081**, the UI is **definitely** still on an old build — repeat steps 2–3.

---

**Summary:** Sign-in fails because the page is calling **localhost:8081**. Redeploy a web build compiled with **`API_BASE_URL=https://api.<your-domain>`**, then **bust cache** (service worker). Verify domain spelling (**medtech** vs **meditech**).

_**Agent (default)**_

Seeing **both** `localhost:8081` and `api.medtechinventorysystem.org` in `main.dart.js` is **normal**. The Dart code still contains the local-dev strings (`_localApiBase`, probe URLs, etc.); the compiler often **keeps those literals** in the bundle even when the running app mostly uses the production URL.

So that `grep` does **not** prove which URL the browser actually uses at login.

---

### What the login error actually means

`Failed to fetch … http://localhost:8081/auth_login.php` means that, in **your browser**, `ApiClient` was given **`http://localhost:8081`** as the base URL (or an **old JS file** that still does that is what’s running).

So you’re chasing either:

1. **Stale app in the browser** (very common with Flutter web + **service worker**), or  
2. **Stale `web` Docker image** (image built before you fixed `build/web`, while disk was already updated), or  
3. Less common: this machine really has something answering **`http://localhost:8081/ping.php`**, so auto-detection “locks in” localhost (only plausible on a dev PC, not a random visitor).

---

### What to do next (in order)

**A. Force the running container to match disk**

On the droplet:

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
docker compose build --no-cache web
docker compose up -d web
```

**B. Break caching in the browser**

For `admin.medtechinventorysystem.org` (use your real host):

- Open DevTools → **Application** → **Service Workers** → **Unregister**  
- **Clear site data** (or at least storage for that origin)  
- Hard reload, or use a **private window**

**C. Confirm what the droplet serves (not just the repo folder)**

```bash
docker compose exec web sh -c 'grep -o "localhost:8081" /usr/share/nginx/html/main.dart.js | head -1'
```

- If that prints `localhost:8081`, the **image** still has an old bundle → fix build context + rebuild `web` (step A).  
- If it prints nothing but `grep` on the **host** `~/inventoryQR/build/web/main.dart.js` still finds `localhost:8081`, the **host folder and image are out of sync** → rebuild `web` after a fresh `git pull` / correct `build/web`.

**D. If it still hits localhost after A–C**

Try the same site in a **browser profile that never opened this app before**. If it works there, it’s **100% old storage/service worker** on the original profile.

---

### Domain typo

Your earlier screenshot used **`admin.meditechinventorysystem.org`** (*meditech*). Your API/config use **`medtech`**. Make sure the URL you open is the one you actually deploy and purge cache **for that exact host**.

---

**Bottom line:** The server repo’s `main.dart.js` can contain **both** strings; your error means the **runtime** (or **cached** JS) is still using **localhost**. Refresh the **`web`** image with `--no-cache`, then **unregister the service worker and clear site data** for that admin hostname.

**1. Rebuild the `web` image without cache**

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
docker compose build --no-cache web
docker compose up -d web
```

**2. Confirm the image actually changed**

```bash
docker compose exec web sh -c 'wc -c /usr/share/nginx/html/main.dart.js'
docker images inventory_web --no-trunc
```

After `--no-cache`, the image **Created** time should be new and `main.dart.js` size may change.

**3. Make sure the files Docker copies are the ones you want**

On the **host** (same machine):

```bash
ls -la ~/inventoryQR/build/web/main.dart.js
# optional: note modification time after git pull
```

If `build/web` on the server is **old** or never got the commit with a proper `flutter build web --release --dart-define=API_BASE_URL=...`, pull/fix that **before** step 1:

```bash
git pull origin main
```

**4. Browser**

After a real rebuild, still **unregister the service worker** and hard-refresh (or use a private window) so the browser doesn’t keep an old `main.dart.js`.

---

**Summary:** `Built 0.0s` = **cache** → same files inside the container. Run **`docker compose build --no-cache web`** (then `up -d web`), ensure **`~/inventoryQR/build/web`** is up to date from Git, then clear **service worker / site data** in the browser.

---

### What to do next

1. **In the browser** (for your real admin hostname): unregister **service workers**, clear **site data** for that origin, then reload (or use a **private window**). Old JS is a common reason login still fails after a good deploy.

2. **Try login again.** If it still fails, open **DevTools → Network**, submit login, and see the **request URL** for `auth_login.php`:
   - If it’s `https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/...` → API/CORS/SSL issue next.
   - If it’s still `http://localhost:8081/...` → still cached client or wrong tab/domain.

3. **Optional sanity check on the server** (only tells you the string exists in the file, not which branch runs):

   ```bash
   docker compose exec web sh -c 'grep -o "https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org" /usr/share/nginx/html/main.dart.js | head -1'
   ```

If that prints the URL, the production string is in the bundle; behavior then depends on **release mode + startup** and **browser cache**.

---

**Bottom line:** Your **rebuild + file sizes** line up. Next step is **browser cache/service worker** and confirming the **Network** tab shows the real API host on login.

The screenshot shows two important details:

## 1. You’re on the **apex** URL, not `admin`

Address bar: **`medtechinventorysystem.org/#/login`** (no `admin.`).

Everything we wired in `nginx.prod.conf` is for hostnames like **`admin.medtechinventorysystem.org`**, **`api.`**, **`db.`**. The **apex** (`medtechinventorysystem.org`) can:

- Hit a **different** DNS target than `admin`, or  
- Hit the **same** server but with **different caching** (browser + **Cloudflare** treat it as another site).

So you can still get an **old** `main.dart.js` that calls **`localhost:8081`**, even after fixing **`admin`** and the Docker image.

**Try exactly:**  
`https://admin.medtechinventorysystem.org/#/login`  
in a **private window** after clearing site data for **that** host (or purge Cloudflare cache for that host).

---

## 2. Old app still running in the browser

The red bar proves the tab is executing JS that still uses **`http://localhost:8081/auth_login.php`**. The container can be correct while **this tab** loads cached JS (service worker / disk cache / **Cloudflare edge cache**).

Do this for the **same host you use in the address bar** (e.g. `medtechinventorysystem.org`):

1. DevTools → **Application** → **Service workers** → **Unregister**  
2. **Clear site data** for that origin  
3. **Cloudflare** → **Caching** → **Purge Everything** (or at least purge `main.dart.js` / `flutter_service_worker.js` for that hostname)

Then reload (or use a **new** browser profile).

---

## 3. If it still says `localhost:8081` after that

Then the runtime is **not** taking the release “production URL” path (or something is still wrong with which build is served). A solid fix in code is to **always set the production API base on web when not in debug**, e.g. in `loadAndApplyBaseUrl()` use production when **`kReleaseMode || (kIsWeb && !kDebugMode)`** so a mis-reported `kReleaseMode` on web can’t send users to localhost.

That requires editing `lib/data/app_config_service.dart`. I’m in **Ask mode** and can’t patch the repo here; switch to **Agent mode** if you want that change applied and a new `build/web` committed for you.

_**Agent (default)**_

You’re on the right stack (**DigitalOcean droplet + Cloudflare in front**). The login error is still **`http://localhost:8081/auth_login.php`**, so the **page your browser runs** is not using the production API URL, even though the server image can contain `https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org` in the same minified file.

Below is how **DO + Cloudflare** fits in, and what to do next.

---

## How traffic should flow

1. **Browser** → **HTTPS** → **Cloudflare** (Singapore edge, etc.)  
2. **Cloudflare** → **your droplet** `159.223.47.185` on **port 80** (HTTP), if you use **Flexible** SSL and nginx only listens on **80** (your `nginx.prod.conf` is HTTP on 80).  
3. **Nginx container** uses `Host:` header → `admin.` → **web** (Flutter static files) or `api.` → **api** (PHP).

So Cloudflare is not “wrong” by default; the problem is **which JS runs in the browser** and **what base URL it sets**.

---

## Cloudflare settings that matter

| Setting | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **SSL/TLS → Flexible** | Nginx on the droplet is only **:80** unless you added TLS there. **Full** makes Cloudflare hit **:443** on the origin and often causes **521** if nothing listens on 443. |
| **DNS A records** | `admin` and `api` (and `db` if used) should point to the **droplet IP**. Orange-cloud proxied is normal. |
| **Caching** | Flutter ships **`main.dart.js`** and a **service worker**. Old copies can keep **`localhost:8081`** behavior. **Purge cache** (at least “Everything” once, or purge by URL for `main.dart.js` / `flutter_service_worker.js`). |

---

## Why you still see `localhost:8081` on `admin.medtech...`

Typical causes (often combined):

1. **Stale JavaScript** – Cloudflare edge cache or browser cache / **service worker** still serving an **older** `main.dart.js`.  
2. **Runtime URL selection** – On some web builds, the app may not take the simple “release → production URL only” path you expect, so it can still end up on **localhost** after detection/settings.

So: **Droplet + Cloudflare can be fine** while the **client** still runs bad JS or bad logic.

**Do this on `admin.medtechinventorysystem.org`:**

- Cloudflare: **Caching → Configuration → Purge Everything** (for that zone, or use single-file purge for the JS URLs you see in Network).  
- Browser: DevTools → **Application** → **Service workers** → **Unregister** → **Clear site data** → hard reload, or a **new profile / incognito**.  
- DevTools → **Network**: reload, open `main.dart.js` → **Response headers** – check **CF-Cache-Status** / **Age** to see if Cloudflare is serving an old object.

---

## Strong fix (code, not only cache)

To stop **any** web build (except local debug) from using localhost for the API, change **`AppConfigService.loadAndApplyBaseUrl()`** so that on **`kIsWeb && !kDebugMode`** you **always** set the base URL to your production API (same as `_productionApiBase`), then rebuild `build/web`, commit, redeploy `web`, purge CF + browser again.

I’m in **Ask mode** and can’t edit the repo; switch to **Agent mode** if you want that patch applied and a fresh `build/web` produced for you.

---

## Quick verification from the droplet (origin, no Cloudflare)

```bash
curl -sI -H "Host: admin.medtechinventorysystem.org" http://127.0.0.1/ | head -5
```

If that returns **200** (or 304), the **droplet + nginx** side is serving the app; remaining issues are **cache + JS/API URL logic**.

---

**Bottom line:** Using **DigitalOcean + Cloudflare** is normal. Your screenshot means the **browser is still executing code that posts login to `localhost:8081`**. Purge **Cloudflare** and **browser/service worker** caches, confirm **Network** shows `auth_login` going to **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`**, and if not, add the **web production URL hardening** in `app_config_service.dart` and redeploy the static bundle.

---

Here’s what was done.

### Code change (`lib/data/app_config_service.dart`)

`loadAndApplyBaseUrl()` now treats **any Flutter web session that is not debug** the same as release:

- **`kReleaseMode || (kIsWeb && !kDebugMode)`** → always set the API base from **`API_BASE_URL`** / default `https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`, and skip localhost probing and prefs for that path.
- **`flutter run -d chrome`** stays on **`kDebugMode`** → your old auto-detect / localhost flow is unchanged.

If `API_BASE_URL` is empty at compile time, it falls back to **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`**.

### Fresh bundle

- Ran **`flutter build web --release --dart-define=API_BASE_URL=https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`**.
- Committed **`lib/data/app_config_service.dart`**, **`build/web/main.dart.js`**, **`build/web/flutter_bootstrap.js`**.
- Pushed to **`main`** as **`c6cea09`** (GitHub Actions deploy should run if configured).

### What you should do

1. **On the droplet** (or wait for CI):
   ```bash
   cd ~/inventoryQR && git pull origin main
   docker compose build --no-cache web && docker compose up -d web
   ```
2. **Cloudflare:** **Purge cache** (at least once after this deploy).
3. **Browser:** For `admin.medtechinventorysystem.org`, **unregister service workers** and **clear site data**, or use a **private window**, then try login again.

After that, login should call **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/auth_login.php`** instead of **`localhost:8081`**.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Checking db connection**

I'm investigating why PDO throws db_error despite healthy container and correct env vars. I found that the database medlab_inventory doesn't exist and needs to be created by the init script. The auth login page fails on DB connection before checking credentials, requiring a working PDO MySQL driver in Docker. I'm diagnosing the DB connection issue by testing PDO connectivity and checking Docker logs. </details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

That JSON means the **API container is up and PHP is running**, but **`db.php` cannot open a PDO connection** to MySQL/MariaDB (any call to a script that `require`s `db.php` will fail the same way—including opening `auth_login.php` in the browser with GET, because the DB connect runs first).

```22:30:c:\3rd Year\SEM 2\SIA 2\QR CODE INVENTORY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM\inventoryQR\inventory_api\db.php
try {
  $pdo = new PDO($dsn, $user, $pass, [
    PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE => PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION,
    PDO::ATTR_DEFAULT_FETCH_MODE => PDO::FETCH_ASSOC,
  ]);
} catch (Throwable $e) {
  http_response_code(500);
  echo json_encode(['error' => 'db_error']);
  exit;
}
```

So this is **not** a Flutter/Cloudflare issue anymore; it’s **API → database** inside Docker.

---

### On the droplet (SSH), run these and check output

**1. Are `db` and `api` healthy?**

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
docker compose ps
docker compose logs db --tail 80
docker compose logs api --tail 40
```

**2. Can MariaDB be used from the API container?**

```bash
docker compose exec api php -r "try { \$p = new PDO('mysql:host=db;port=3306;dbname=medlab_inventory;charset=utf8mb4','root','', [PDO::ATTR_ERRMODE=>PDO::ERRMODE_EXCEPTION]); echo \"OK\n\"; } catch (Throwable \$e) { echo \$e->getMessage(), \"\n\"; }"
```

- If you see **`OK`**, DB is reachable from `api` (then we’d look at schema/users next).  
- If you see an error message, that text is the real cause (refused, access denied, unknown database, etc.).

**3. Is MariaDB itself OK?**

```bash
docker compose exec db mysqladmin ping -h localhost -uroot
docker compose exec db mysql -uroot -e "SHOW DATABASES;"
```

---

### Typical causes after prunes / rebuilds

| Situation | What to do |
|-----------|------------|
| **`db` restarting / unhealthy / OOM** on 512 MB | Check `docker compose logs db`; may need swap or fewer services / bigger droplet. |
| **Fresh volume, init didn’t finish** | Wait for `db` **healthy**, then `docker compose restart api`. |
| **Wrong env on `api`** | `docker compose exec api env \| grep DB_` — expect `DB_HOST=db`, `DB_NAME=medlab_inventory`, `DB_USER=root`, `DB_PASS` empty. |
| **Database missing** | `SHOW DATABASES` should list `medlab_inventory`. |

---

### After DB works

`auth_login.php` expects a **POST** with JSON `username` / `password`; a browser **GET** will usually return **`missing_fields`** (400), not prove login—use the app or `curl -X POST ...` once DB is fixed.

Run the **`php -r` PDO test** and, if you can, paste the **exact error string** or the **`db` container logs**; that pinpoints the next step.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Fixing auth middleware**

I'm investigating why MariaDB rejects root user with empty password despite MYSQL_ALLOW_EMPTY_PASSWORD being set to 'yes'. 

**Investigating db healthcheck**

I'm checking why the DB shows as healthy despite potential access issues with mysqladmin ping. The healthcheck uses `mysqladmin ping -h localhost` without user, which might connect via unix socket instead of TCP. The healthcheck passes when mysqladmin shows 'ose shows healthy', suggesting it may be using a different connection path than expected. The root cause is likely a persistent volume from a previous MariaDB instance with mismatched root user auth settings. The root cause is socket auth vs TCP connections - `root@localhost` uses unix_socket while `root@%` requires password authentication. 

**Setting root password**

I'm setting explicit MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD to fix persistent auth issues from old volume data. I'm adding explicit MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD to docker-compose and matching DB_PASS in api service. 

I'll use environment variable substitution with a default value in docker-compose and document the reset process in README. </details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Diagnosing: MariaDB is rejecting `root` with an empty password — usually caused by a persistent `db_data` volume initialized under different settings, or MariaDB 10.4+ auth for remote vs. localhost. Setting an explicit root password in Docker Compose and matching `DB_PASS` / phpMyAdmin.

_**Agent (default)**_

Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.

## Cause

MariaDB was logging **`Access denied for user 'root'@'...' (using password: NO)`**. Your API and PHP use **`DB_PASS: ""`**, but the data in **`db_data`** no longer matches “root with no password” (common after image/volume changes or MariaDB auth behavior).  
**`ping.php` does not use the database**, so the API could stay “healthy” while anything that loads **`db.php`** returned **`db_error`**.

## Repo changes (pushed as `b5d0496`)

1. **`docker-compose.yml`**
   - Set **`MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: ${MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD:-medlab_root_change_me}`** on **`db`**.
   - Set **`DB_PASS`** and **`PMA_PASSWORD`** to the **same** value.
   - Removed **`MYSQL_ALLOW_EMPTY_PASSWORD`**.
   - Healthcheck uses **`mysqladmin ping`** with that password.

2. **`.env.example`** – documents **`MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD`**.

3. **`README.md`** – short note + **one-time volume reset** steps.

---

## What you must do on the droplet

Changing compose **does not** change the password inside an **existing** MariaDB volume. You need a **one-time** reset (this **wipes the DB**; `medlab_inventory.sql` will load again on init):

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
git pull origin main

docker compose down
docker volume ls | grep db_data    # confirm name, often inventoryqr_db_data
docker volume rm inventoryqr_db_data

docker compose up -d --build
```

Optional but recommended: create **`~/inventoryQR/.env`**:

```bash
echo 'MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=your_strong_secret_here' > ~/inventoryQR/.env
```

If you use `.env`, put the **same** value you want for root; **`docker compose`** will pass it to **`db`**, **`api`**, and **phpMyAdmin**. If you skip `.env`, the default is **`medlab_root_change_me`** (change it for anything public).

**Verify:**

```bash
docker compose exec api php -r "new PDO('mysql:host=db;dbname=medlab_inventory;charset=utf8mb4','root','medlab_root_change_me'); echo \"OK\n\";"
```

(Use your real password if you set one in `.env`.)

Then try **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/ping.php`** and login from the admin app again.

---

_**User**_

.env=MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=congtv123
DB_PORT=3306

_**Agent (default)**_

Use a **`.env` file** in the project root with **exactly** these lines (no `*.env=` prefix on the first line):

```env
MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=congtv123
DB_PORT=3306
```

`docker-compose.yml` already reads **`${MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD:-...}`** and **`${DB_PORT:-3306}`**, so that’s enough on the droplet at **`~/inventoryQR/.env`**.

**After changing `.env`**, MariaDB still uses the password stored **inside the volume** until you recreate it. If you haven’t already:

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
docker compose down
docker volume rm inventoryqr_db_data   # adjust name if `docker volume ls` shows something else
docker compose up -d --build
```

**Security:** You shared **`congtv123` in chat**. Treat it as **compromised**: pick a **new** strong password in `.env`, recreate the volume again (or `ALTER USER` in MySQL), and don’t commit **`.env`** (it should stay in `.gitignore`).

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Checking password mismatch**

The PDO test failed because the PHP script used a hardcoded password instead of the one from .env. The database connection is failing because the test uses `medlab_root_change_me` while .env contains `congtv123`, and .env wasn't loaded during docker-compose up. The test fails because .env isn't loaded during docker-compose up, causing MariaDB to use `congtv123` instead of `medlab_root_change_me`. </details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

That error is almost certainly a **password mismatch in your test**, not a broken stack.

You ran PHP with:

`... 'root','medlab_root_change_me'`

but on the server your **`.env`** has:

`MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=congtv123`

So MariaDB was initialized with **`congtv123`**, while the one-liner used the **compose default**. MySQL then correctly returns **1045 Access denied (using password: YES)**.

### Check what the API container actually uses

```bash
docker compose exec api env | grep '^DB_PASS='
docker compose exec db env | grep '^MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD='
```

### Verify PDO with the **same** password as `DB_PASS`

Either put your real password in the command:

```bash
docker compose exec api php -r "new PDO('mysql:host=db;dbname=medlab_inventory;charset=utf8mb4','root','congtv123'); echo \"OK\n\";"
```

Or avoid echoing the password in shell history by using env inside PHP:

```bash
docker compose exec api php -r '$p = getenv("DB_PASS"); new PDO("mysql:host=db;dbname=medlab_inventory;charset=utf8mb4", "root", $p); echo "OK\n";'
```

(`DB_PASS` is what `inventory_api/db.php` uses.)

### If it **still** fails after using the right password

1. Confirm **`.env` lives in `~/inventoryQR`** and you ran **`docker compose up`** from that directory (Compose loads `.env` from the project dir).
2. If you ever brought the stack up **without** `.env` and then added `.env`, the **volume might have been created with the default password** — then either remove the volume once and `up` again with `.env` in place, or align the password with whatever was used at first init.

**Summary:** Don’t use `medlab_root_change_me` in the test if `.env` says `congtv123`; use **`congtv123`** or the **`getenv("DB_PASS")`** snippet above.


_**Agent (default)**_

Here’s what your output means and how to fix the last step.

## 1. PDO with `medlab_root_change_me` failed — expected

Your `.env` sets **`MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=congtv123`**, so MariaDB was **never** initialized with `medlab_root_change_me`. Always test with **`congtv123`** or use:

`docker compose exec api php -r '$p=getenv("DB_PASS"); new PDO("mysql:host=db;dbname=medlab_inventory;charset=utf8mb4","root",$p); echo "OK\n";'`

## 2. `{"error":"invalid_credentials"}` is good for infra

That response means:

- PHP reached the DB  
- It ran the login query  
- **`admin` / `YOUR_ACTUAL_PASSWORD` is wrong** (placeholder isn’t a real password)

## 3. Seed data: default admin user

From your `medlab_inventory.sql`:

- **Username:** `admin`  
- **Password:** `admin` (stored **in plain text** — the API compares with `!==` on the raw string)

So a **clean** test (one line, no line breaks) is:

```bash
curl -sS -X POST 'https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/auth_login.php' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"username":"admin","password":"admin"}'
```

You should see JSON with **`"ok":true`** if nothing else is wrong.

Your earlier **`missing_fields`** and **`curl: (3) URL rejected`** came from a **broken multiline / pasted** command (shell saw stray quotes and junk), not from the API.

## 4. phpMyAdmin

Your [phpMyAdmin `medlab_inventory` structure](https://db.medtechinventorysystem.org/index.php?route=/database/structure&db=medlab_inventory) matches that: you can open the **`users`** table and confirm the **`admin`** row’s **`password`** column is `admin`.

**Important:** `db.medtech...` is on the public internet with **root** access via phpMyAdmin — restrict access (Cloudflare **Access**, IP allowlist, VPN, or disable when not needed) and plan to **rotate** `MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD` and app passwords since they’ve been shared.

---

**Summary:** DB and API are working. Use **`admin` / `admin`** from the seed SQL, or set a new password in phpMyAdmin in the **`password`** field for that user. Use a **single-line** `curl` like above to avoid paste errors.

---

{"ok":true,"username":"admin","email":"admin@inventory.com","role":"admin"}root@ubuntu-s-1vcpu-512mb-10gb-sgp1-01:~/inventoryQR#

_**Agent (default)**_

That response means the **API + database + login logic** are all working end-to-end:

- **`{"ok":true,"username":"admin",...}`** — `admin` / `admin` is valid for your current seed data.

You can use the same credentials on **`https://admin.medtechinventorysystem.org`**. If the site still misbehaves, clear cache / service worker for that host; the backend is fine.

**Next steps (optional):** change the **`admin`** password in phpMyAdmin ( **`users`** table ) and use something stronger than the default; rotate **`MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD`** if it was ever exposed; lock down **`db.medtech...`** (Cloudflare Access, IP rules, or turn it off when you don’t need it).

_**Agent (default)**_

Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause
Something in the web app was still calling **`ApiClient.setBaseUrl(...)`** with **`http://localhost:8081`** (auto-detect / prefs / old bundle). Once that was set, every login used **`localhost`** in the browser, which always fails.

### Fix (in the repo, pushed as **`2a2804d`**)
In **`lib/data/api_client.dart`**, on **`kIsWeb`** we now:

- **Drop** any base URL that contains **`localhost`** or **`127.0.0.1`** in **`setBaseUrl`**
- In **`_baseUrl()`**, treat those the same and fall back to **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org`**

So even an old or wrong config can’t send login to **`localhost`** on web.

A new **`build/web`** was produced and pushed with **`flutter build web --release --dart-define=API_BASE_URL=...`**.

---

### What you should run on the droplet

```bash
cd ~/inventoryQR
git pull origin main
docker compose build --no-cache web
docker compose up -d web
```

### In the browser (important)
1. **`admin.medtechinventorysystem.org`** → DevTools → **Application** → **Service Workers** → **Unregister**
2. **Clear site data** for that site (or use a **new private window**)
3. **Cloudflare** → **Caching** → **Purge Everything** (or purge `main.dart.js` / `flutter_service_worker.js` for that host)

Then open **`https://admin.medtechinventorysystem.org/#/login`** and sign in with **`admin` / `admin`** (or your real user).

---

**Check:** After deploy, this should show **no** localhost (or only inside unrelated strings):

```bash
docker compose exec web sh -c 'grep -o "http://localhost:8081/auth_login" /usr/share/nginx/html/main.dart.js | head -1'
```

Login requests in **Network** should go to **`https://api.medtechinventorysystem.org/auth_login.php`**.
